posts.caption:
London, this is your moment.
Chopova Lowena — the cult London-based label known for its rebellious mix of traditional craft influences, punk spirit, a playful, almost “cute” energy, and instantly recognisable carabiner skirts — is opening up its archive for a rare one-day sample sale.
Expect one-off pieces, past-season gems, and exclusive finds you won’t see again. It’s the kind of drop that defines why the brand has built such a devoted following — playful, subversive, and impossible to replicate.
Happening tomorrow only, with restocks throughout the day and strictly limited quantities (yes, one carabiner skirt per person).
No tickets, just show up.
And if you needed one more reason — this won’t happen again until 2028.
See you in the queue.

posts.caption:
@chadmooreholla_ is best known for his tender, spontaneous pictures of New York’s bright young things, but the American photographer’s latest photo book is a departure from his usual portraits. ⁠
⁠
As the title suggests, Eyes and Skies (published by @superlabo), moves between the micro and the macro as it presents his photographs of sunsets and sunrises, skylines and starry nights alongside his close-up studies of eyes. ⁠
⁠
Moore first turned his lens skyward during Covid, creating his previous book Anybody Anyway – a monograph combining his dreamy portraits with pictures of the cosmos. In a sense, Eyes and Skies picks up where Anybody Anyway left off – contemplating the mysteries of the universe. ⁠
⁠
“I started doing the sky images quite slowly, as I matured with making photos and got bored with waiting around for something to happen. Most of my images rely on someone else – typically my friends,” he tells @ohnoitsemilyd. “I just had the thought, ‘What has the same energy as an emotional portrait of a human subject?’, and I figured the closest thing would be the stars.” Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Eyes and Skies by @chadmooreholla_⁠
⁠
#ChadMoore⁠

posts.caption:
John Waters, the self-styled “Pope of Trash”, turns 80 today. ⁠
⁠
He’s the man who spent his teenage years taking LSD in the suburbs of Baltimore with Divine, who he later directed to eat dog shit in the cult film Pink Flamingoes. He’s also the author of the single best piece of romantic advice ever given: “If you go home with someone and they don’t have any books, don’t fuck them.” So with the latter quip in mind, we looked back on his interview with #CathClarke featured in the S/S14 issue of AnOther Magazine.⁠
⁠
From dealing with bullies to how to stay young, he offers an unconventional guide to life. Read it at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #JohnWaters. Photography by @mauricehaasphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of standing, magazine and text that says 'AnOther John Waters Waters on Staying Young, Regrets and More'.

posts.caption:
Romanticising our past is part of how families tell stories, but photographer and filmmaker @tirtharabinlawati set that aside when he returned to Nepal with stylist @samtthapa, focusing instead on the country’s changing youth culture.⁠
⁠
In 2025, the pair travelled back to the country of their births to create Where We Meet, a project spanning two series shown at Solar Beam Kingdom, curated by Skylar Kang and Jeremy Chen: Girls Skate Nepal, on young girls reshaping skate culture in Kathmandu, and Jamkabhet, tracing underground music scenes and youth communities. ⁠
⁠
“As someone of Nepalese descent but having grown up predominantly in the UK, I’ve always wanted to understand and connect with what the youth were up to over there,” says Thapa. @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with the duo about the project at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Where We Meet. Photography by @tirtharabinlawati. Styling by @samtthapa

posts.caption:
In 1977, in Minneapolis, a 19-year-old musician named Prince Rogers Nelson caught the attention of music agent Owen Husney and his partner Gary Levinson. Keen to have him photographed, they approached @robertwhitmanphoto, playing an early demo of Soft and Wet to draw him in. It worked. Whitman was transfixed, and soon after photographed Nelson, who would become the artist known as Prince.⁠
⁠
Ten years after his passing, we revisit those images collected in Prince Pre Fame, a book that gathers the full series. As Whitman puts it, it’s “a book that every Prince fan should have on their table, telling the story of two guys from Minneapolis following their passions, trying to pursue their dreams.”⁠
⁠
📸 Prince Pre Fame. Photography @robertwhitmanphoto

posts.caption:
For #BazaarIndia’s 17th anniversary issue, actor Ananya Panday (@AnanyaPanday) shares that she loves being alone now. “I used to be someone who couldn’t be alone at all. I’d be scared to sit alone for even 10 minutes. I always needed someone around me — my partner, my parents, my friends,” admits Panday. “But when I started living alone, even though I’m in the same building as my parents, that little bit of space changed everything. Building your own environment and doing things your own way has shifted my love for space.”
Much like our cover installation, which conveys the negotiation of the fragile space between two people, the artwork by How Are You Feeling Studio features two film cranes facing each other like figures. Their machine bodies, covered in soft fabric, suggest an unexpected intimacy.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

Cities aren’t inaccessible by accident—they’re designed that way.
After a life-changing injury, Prateek Khandelwal (@prateekkhandelwal5) began rethinking how cities are built and who they’re built for. Through Ramp My City (@rampmycity), he’s working on the ground to make public spaces usable for people with disabilities.
600+ spaces made accessible. One clear goal:
design cities that work for everyone.

Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.

“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”
Continue reading Charlee Fraser’s interview with BAZAAR at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Nike faces backlash; Chanel picks Elordi — plus other key industry moves.
1. @Moncler reported a 12 percent sales increase in the first quarter, driven by strong demand in Asia, particularly China and Korea.
2. @Gap’s collaboration with @VictoriaBeckham garnered instant buzz online, with several pieces — which run from $34 to $328 — selling out within a few hours.
3. Laila Gohar (@lailacooks)’s reworked antique merry-go-round for @Arket was among the standout activations from fashion brands at the Salone del Mobile design fair Milan, with other notable installations presented by @Gucci and @LoroPiana.
4. Brands that implemented tariff-induced price hikes, including @ElfCosmetics and @Lululemon, are facing class action lawsuits from consumers hoping to recoup their losses after the US government opened a tariff refund portal for businesses this week.
5. Bernard Arnault reaffirmed his confidence in @LVMH at the company’s annual general meeting this week, even as stagnant sales, a tumbling share price and questions over succession have dented its image.
6. @Chanelofficial named “Euphoria” star Jacob Elordi as the new face of Bleu de Chanel, replacing Timothée Chalamet, who was named as an ambassador in 2023. Elordi’s first campaign will drop in May.
7. Advocacy group The Clean Clothes Campaign and four Dutch consumers are suing @Levis, alleging the company misled consumers on the ethics of its labour practices.
8. @Lululemon dismissed activist investor Elliott International’s wishes to select Ralph Lauren veteran Jane Nielsen as its new CEO, instead picking @Nike executive Heidi O’Neill. The news sent its stock price to a 52-week low.
9. @Nike’s latest attempt to recapture the marketing zeitgeist hit a roadblock after its ad for the Boston Marathon, which read "Runners welcome. Walkers tolerated,” faced backlash for “pace shaming.” The brand quickly removed the ad.
This Week in Fashion is BoF’s weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

The challenge for many brands is understanding the tipping point between unveiling too much or too little.
BoF’s Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores how brands can build anticipation without giving everything away too soon. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Luxury entered 2026 with hopes that new creative directors and signs of stabilisation would finally help the sector turn a corner. Instead, the latest round of earnings has raised bigger questions about what growth now looks like for the industry. While brands including Dior, Gucci and Chanel are generating renewed interest, that excitement has not yet translated into a meaningful sales rebound.
From the slowing Chinese market to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, luxury conglomerates are facing a complex web of challenges that creative hype alone cannot solve.
On the episode, BoF luxury editors Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) and Robert Williams (@robert.williams.writes) explain why China remains such a critical missing piece, why Louis Vuitton is under closer scrutiny than usual, and why jewellery continues to outperform the rest of luxury.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Unsplash

the walls were covered end to end in peonies, goldenrod, dahlias, carnations, delphiniums, and roses. the collection began with a masterfully tailored black suit: a take on the iconic bar suit, more artful interpretations followed. it spoke volumes in the direction he took dior to: structured, feminine, and sensibly simple. a vivid red belted coat-dress was absolutely brilliant. mostly comprised of strapless and structured gowns the eveningwear was magnificent. a bright yellow gown with a translucent top was glorious along with a green-black strapless gown which was a sterling ruby painting.
simons never struck me as a minimalist, his every look is filled with detailed grandeur and richness that simply is imperceptible to many. what was fascinating about this show is that how with a heritage-rich brand like dior, raf wasn’t overly lost in history and vintage aesthetic but infused his much-needed modernity and pragmatism.
rare moments in history present us with a collection in which each and every look is better than the one before and yes this was one of them. classic christian dior silhouettes paired with the modernity of raf simons was as iconic as it was necessary.

posts.caption:
Curator Jeffrey Uslip wants everyone to know that everything is normal.
"This is the smoothest exhibition I've curated in 30 years," said Uslip, who has been working on the US Pavilion at the @venice.art.biennale 2026, which opens in two weeks.
Uslip was insistent on the point. "I also just want to be clear, because I know how people talk, it's important to know that we have had complete artistic autonomy throughout this process," he said by phone. Later, by email, he emphasized the process to select the artist — the sculptor almaallen — was "exactly the same" as it always was.
There's a reason Uslip was adamant. This year's US Pavilion, which is partly funded by the government, has been plagued by controversies and delays. For months, it was uncertain whether the State Department, whose Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs oversees the search, would select an artist to present in Venice at all.
Swipe to read more, and see the full story at link in bio.
📸: File Photo/Elisabetta Villa/Getty Images | Beowulf Sheehan | Ana Hop | Spencer Wells | Ed Mumford

posts.caption:
Ten years ago today, @beyonce released her pioneering visual album "Lemonade," which solidified the singer and songwriter as one of the most creative and audacious pop stars of her generation. The project also inspired countless trends, including the "Lemonade braids" she wore on the album's cover, her first experimentation with country music in "Daddy Lessons" and a banner season for Red Lobster after she name dropped the chain restaurant in "Formation."
One particular look may define the album: the mustard @roberto_cavalli dress Beyoncé wears to take a retaliatory baseball bat to parked cars, a fire hydrant, a piñata, and shop windows in her fevered, plucky ode to her lover's betrayal, "Hold Up." The slightly sheer cascade of ruffles, with a thin lace of straps at the neck, hardly grabbed attention when it debuted in the Fall 2016 Roberto Cavalli show, which was inspired by 1970s rock 'n roll glamour.
Stylist @bcompleted plucked it from countless yellow dresses however, and on a grinning, retaliatory Beyoncé, its louche frills transformed into a statement of feminine power, freedom and vengeance.
📸: @beyonce; @parkwood

posts.caption:
"I was there 19 months," @sabato.desarno said of @gucci, "but I've worked in fashion for 23 years."
After a short but widely scrutinized tenure as creative director at the fashion house — where his restrained aesthetic struggled to gain commercial traction during a broader downturn in the luxury sector — he has been moving across the worlds of design and art with new projects at a more deliberate pace.
Now, during @milan.design.week, he is curating "INSIEME," an exhibition bringing together twelve Italian artisanal companies.
As De Sarno puts it: "I kept thinking about how, when you go to an exhibition or a fair, what you usually see are finished objects. What interested me with 'INSIEME' was turning the attention on everything that comes before that, everything that leads up to the final piece: the mistakes, the decisions, the timing, and those moments when you have to choose a direction and can't go back."
Asked what comes next, De Sarno said: "I see myself simply as Sabato. Someone with different interests, creating conversations with people who are experts in their fields. I'm not an expert in art or design. What I've enjoyed is entering into those conversations and letting projects emerge from them. I don't really feel the need to define it with a title."
📸 : Agnese Bedini/DSL Studio

posts.caption:
For teens during the decade, soundtrack albums were must-have merchandise, PARENTAL ADVISORY labels be damned.
The same year as @bazluhrmann's "Romeo + Juliet," "Trainspotting" came out and @iggypopofficial thumping "Lust for Life" set the pace for another CD that was both achingly cool and a massive commercial success. This was the era of "Dangerous Minds" (1995), "Good Will Hunting" (1997), "Cruel Intentions" (1999), and many more great albums that those of a certain age remember as clearly as the movies themselves.
The "Romeo + Juliet" album itself peaked at No.2 on the Billboard 200 chart and went multi-platinum in several countries. Meanwhile, as Marius de Vries, co-music producer on the film points out, films in the '90s were "regularly spawning enormous hit singles." Think @whitneyhouston's "I Will Always Love You" ("The Bodyguard," 1992) or @celinedion's "My Heart Will Go On" (@titanicmovie, 1997).
In the next decade, the rise of online streaming and death of CDs marked the end of the film soundtrack as we knew it. And while the current wave of '90s nostalgia is partly cyclical, it also taps into a longing for a "much more unified mass culture," said @robharvilla, host of the podcast, "60 Songs That Explain the '90s." In the fragmented internet age, "you don't have that same feeling of unity, where everybody is listening to 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' or 'You Outta Know' at the exact same time."
Without a doubt, the '90s were "a bunch of very golden years," de Vries said. "At the time, we were just having a lot of fun, and I think possibly that was reflected in the music we were making."

posts.caption:
The editorial moves through a series of characters rather than telling one clear story. Every image feels like a different version of the same idea, shifting between moods, identities, and expressions.
In “Una Canzone” for Vogue Italia (October 2018), Luigi & Iango take inspiration from Mina Mazzini, one of Italy’s most iconic singers. She wasn’t just known for her voice, but for turning her entire image into a form of expression, and that energy runs through the whole story.
The cast, including Guinevere van Seenus, Remington Williams, Karen Elson, and Natasa Vojnovic, constantly transform. One moment the looks are clean and sculptural, the next more exaggerated or playful. The erased brows and strong eye shapes by Erin Parsons reference Mina’s signature beauty, but feel more modern and experimental here.
Styling by Patti Wilson mixes classic couture with unexpected elements. Ruffles, bold silhouettes, and unusual proportions create a balance between past and future. Hair by Luigi Murenu adds to that transformation, sometimes subtle, sometimes more dramatic, like the bearded look that still feels surprisingly natural.
The more you look at it, the more it starts to make sense. It’s less about one clear message and more about capturing the mindset of an artist who was always pushing things further.

posts.caption:
Madonna’s style has always leaned toward the bold, but it’s rarely careless. Her looks are usually clearly thought through, sometimes provocative, sometimes more restraine.
Over time, they’ve become less about fashion alone and more about how she presents herself in a frame.
One of the more notable examples is the cemetery shoot. The contrast is immediate: lingerie layered under a trench coat, heels, sheer tights, and flowers in hand, all set against a graveyard. It’s an unusual combination, and it creates a strong visual clash between setting and styling. Still, it doesn’t come across as random. The whole scene feels arranged, like she’s fully aware of the tension it creates and is working with it rather than against it.
Then there’s the red background image with the black sequined dress. It’s much cleaner in comparison. The silhouette is simple, the styling is minimal, with short blonde hair and a steady, direct pose. There’s no real attempt at excess here. Instead, it feels precise and structured, the kind of image that stands out because of its clarity.
The black leather outfit takes an even more stripped-back approach. Gloves, heels, fishnets, sunglasses, all fairly straightforward pieces, worn with a relaxed confidence. She’s seated, composed, and the attitude does most of the work. Nothing feels added for effect.
Across these different looks, what remains consistent is the way she presents them. The clothes change, but the sense of control doesn’t.

posts.caption:
In Tim Walker’s “Check-Mate” editorial for Vogue Italia (December 2015), everything feels a bit unreal, like stepping into a strange version of a fairytale.
Edie Campbell moves through different sets that look like they’re taken from a dream. There are chessboards, oversized props, bold colors, and details that feel slightly off, but in a way that still looks visually right. It’s playful, but not in a childish way, more like a mix between fantasy and high fashion.
The clothes shift between structured couture and more exaggerated, almost costume-like pieces. Some looks are very clean and tailored, others are more dramatic, but they all fit into the same world. Nothing feels random, even when it looks unusual.
Hair and makeup stand out in a subtle but important way. Light brows, strong eye looks, and unexpected color choices make everything feel a bit more surreal without taking attention away from the clothes.
Overall, it’s the kind of editorial that doesn’t try too hard to explain itself. It’s more about the mood, the visuals, and how everything comes together naturally.

posts.caption:
At Alessandro Michele’s Gucci FW2018 Cyborg show, during Milan Fashion Week, the runway was presented inside a „highly controlled“, clinical environment designed to resemble an operating theater. The set was defined by sterile green-turquoise walls, harsh overhead lighting, stainless surfaces, and hospital-style beds, creating a setting that felt detached from traditional fashion spectacle and closer to a staged experiment.
Within this environment, models moved at a measured pace through the space, each look constructed with extreme density and precision. The collection itself merged multiple visual languages at once: historical tailoring, ecclesiastical references, sportswear influences, and highly decorative elements were layered together in a way that resisted any single interpretation. Accessories and props extended the garments into sculptural forms, including hyper-realistic replicas of human heads and hybrid, creature-like objects that disrupted the boundary between body and object.
Beauty direction reinforced this constructed logic. Hair was sculptural and intentionally artificial in finish, while makeup emphasized transformation rather than enhancement, pushing the idea that identity here is something assembled rather than inherent.
Seen in hindsight, the collection aligns closely with contemporary conversations around identity as a layered, mediated construct, anticipating the visual and conceptual codes that now dominate digital fashion culture.

posts.caption:
Nina Hagen is a German singer, songwriter, and actress who first appeared in East Berlin before rising to fame in the late 1970s punk scene. She became known for her extreme voice, theatrical performances, and completely unpredictable public image. Early on she worked as an actress and later moved into music, where she mixed punk, opera, and performance art in a way nobody else was really doing at the time. She quickly became one of the most recognizable and unconventional figures in European punk culture and is still often called the “Godmother of Punk.” ￼
What makes Nina Hagen stand out is not just her music, but her personality. She has always been loud, expressive, and completely unfiltered, often mixing political, spiritual, and social ideas into her public presence. She never really fit into one category, which is exactly what made her interesting in the first place. ￼
In terms of feminism and fashion, Nina Hagen became an early symbol of anti-conformity. In a scene that was already breaking rules, she pushed it even further by using her body, voice, and style as a form of resistance. Punk fashion in general was about rejecting mainstream beauty standards, and she embodied that fully with exaggerated looks, bold makeup, and constantly changing identities. ￼
Her impact on feminism isn’t about slogans, but about presence. She showed that a woman in music didn’t have to be polished, quiet, or “acceptable” to be powerful. Her entire aesthetic and attitude became part of a wider movement where women in punk were not just performers, but central forces shaping the culture itself.

The real challenge?

Because at its core, this industry has never been about product.
It’s always been about the dream.
If you’re in the diamond business, 2026 isn’t about waiting for recovery.

posts.caption:
“Who is the Bleu de Chanel man?” Chanel asked yesterday on IG, as a silhouetted figure of their new fragrance ambassador appeared on their feed.
Well, it definitely wasn’t Timothée Chalamet, who took on the Bleu de Chanel ambassador role in 2023 after the fragrance’s first ever ambassador, Gaspard Ulliel. Ulliel had held the role for twelve years from Bleu de Chanel’s release in 2010 to his tragic death in 2022.
This morning, it was confirmed that Jacob Elordi would replace Chalamet. The announcement features various shots of the actor in profile, highlighting his famously sharp jawline and shaggy mullet. The clip ends on a shot of him pointing straight into the camera with two fingers. In other words, it’s highly masculine, authoritative, and a bit playful in contrast to the minimalistic and soft announcement for Chalamet just a few years ago.
It makes sense: Elordi has built an illustrious reputation that transcends his breakout TV role in Euphoria. He has an Oscar nom under his belt, has starred alongside Margot Robbie, who is also a Chanel ambassador and was featured with Elordi in an ad for Chanel No. 5 last year. Chanel describes him as “a deeply determined man, driven by his instinct.”
For reference, Chalamet’s announcement in 2023 was much less specific, stating only that Bleu was “a fragrance for the man who is deeply himself.” Perhaps that was Chalamet’s selling point at the time. Then again, perhaps that was Bleu de Chanel’s selling point at the time. What changed?
Since Chalamet’s appointment at Chanel, he graduated from the boyish, French-ish heartthrob with the pretty loose curls and the gender-bending outfits, becoming instead a devil-may-care troublemaker with a taste for hype styling and awkward facial hair. And, the charm that made him beloved in his early career all but wore off completely when he made incendiary comments about opera and ballet last February. Chanel, one Threads user notes, is a “major patron” of the Paris Opera Ballet. Could these be related?
Further, will two or three stints be the norm for Chanel’s fragrance ambassadorships? Or can Ulliel’s longstanding role be repeated with Elordi?
In any case, Congrats Jacob!

posts.caption:
The Devil Wears Prada 2 press tour was packed with some of Anne Hathaway and Meryl Streep’s most fabulous fashion moments to date, culminating in an ultra chic NYC premier earlier this week. But the big DWP news now is that Sydney Sweeney was axed from the film.
In the long awaited sequel, Runway magazine is in trouble. So, Miranda Preistley must bring together her trusty team to get things back in order. That team includes her right-hand man Nigel Kipling, her former assistant Andy Sachs, and, lastly, Emily Charlton, who is now the head of Dior US. In the cut scene, Nigel, Andy, and Miranda would have descended upon the Dior offices to enlist Emily’s help, only to find her knee-deep in a fitting with a celebrity. That celebrity would have been Sydney Sweeney as herself.
Entertainment Weekly reports that the scene was edited out because it “did not work structurally.” But, the decision to nix Sweeney comes at a pretty coincidental moment. She is still in hot water after her eugenics-ridden American Eagle ad last year, and has only leaned into the controversy in subsequent interviews. Since then, she has courted more controversy gleefully, staging a faux stunt at the Hollywood sign to promote her lingerie line and playing a provocative role in Euphoria season 3. Her actions have been lauded by Trump and she appears to have become an unofficial symbol of ideal femininity for conservative white-supremacist men.
Even before the news of her cut DWP2 scene, audiences have been speculating on the toxicity of Sweeney’s brand. The actor often wears vintage pieces on red carpets, causing fashion insiders to believe that brands refuse to dress her. Others called for the cancellation of Euphoria after Sweeney appeared in a pornographic baby outfit.
It’s a little hard to believe that Sweeney was cut from DWP2 because of structural issues. Then again, we are talking about Runway mag, where Miranda Preistley once canceled the autumn jacket shoot to pull up the Sedona shoot. And we all know how much THAT cost.

posts.caption:
The fashions seen over the last two Coachella weekends were far from the eccentric, highly curated, culturally-appropriative bohemian glam looks we expected to see. In fact, they were the total opposite.
Stars like Kendall Jenner, Hailey Baldwin, Amber Rose, and Vanessa Hudgens wrote the playbook for how to dress at Coachella: Feathers in your hair, tribal headdresses and flower crowns, macrame crop-tops, and Navajo prints. Coachella style was deeply problematic, it was plastered all over Tumblr, and it was so codified that it became its own retail category: the annual “festival collection.”
But now, the same celebs are turning against it. Kendall Jenner was seen this year in basic tank tops and baggy jorts. Katy Perry and Justin Trudeau’s white t-shirt stunned the internet. And Hailey Beiber, the first lady of Beiberchella, wore only a vintage windbreaker one weekend, and a hoodie with running shorts the next weekend.
There is a lot influencing this: We spotted indie sleaze style reflected in Gabbriette’s Chrome Hearts looks. Amber Rose’s shorts, Hailey Bieber’s and Emma Chamberlain’s leather boots, and Amelia Gray’s stiff straight-cut denim all echoed fashion’s growing interest in workwear. And, the quiet luxury movement informed almost all these outfits being that each came with surreptitious designer tags.
But also, this movement toward underdressing took place under the giant YouTube screen of Beiberchella, which was, by the way, similarly minimal and ad hoc. Erego, this isn’t just a fashion issue (it never is!) but is indicative of a larger trend that is skeptical of overproduction and is nostalgic for authenticity. Perhaps it’s a foil to the onslaught of product placements, highly styled appearances, and manufactured moments that have overtaken Coachella.
Celebrity underdressers appear to be offering counterpoint, a way to distance themselves from the masses who are clawing for visibility at music fests. Or perhaps they’re reaching for the styles that made music festivals so iconic in the first place, back when it was common to be a little messy. Dirty, even. A la Kate Moss at Glastonbury. Is Coachella underdressing actually a breath of fresh air?

posts.caption:
Hallmark Channel dropped the promo poster for its new movie ‘All’s Fair in Love and Mahjong’ on Friday and the internet wasted no time dragging it.
For a movie centered around mahjong, people are side-eyeing the casting choices. The poster shows a mostly white cast, with just one Asian actor, Yan-Kay Crystal Lowe, who is of Chinese-Scottish descent.
The rom-com synopsis reads: “A school nurse turns her love of the game Mahjong into a new path, inspired by a jovial contractor fixing up her home, who encourages her to take a chance on the future.”
Meanwhile, the mahjong tiles featured on the poster are from the film’s branded set, which Hallmark plans to sell on its website through a collab with Miss Mahjong. The Kansas-based game company says it was founded by “a trio of Mahjong-obsessed friends and neighbors” named Anne Marie, Morgan, and Betsy. Although the founders don’t share additional details (🧐) the optics are giving the same energy as a the whitewashed mahjong brands we previously called out: Oh My Mahjong! (just three weeks ago) and The Mahjong Line back in 2021.
The film’s title, a spin on the phrase, “all’s fair in love and war,” was probably meant to be cheeky, but instead the comments section seems ready for battle with the network.
“Just do monopoly next time or something,” wrote one person. “Collective ancestral sigh,” wrote another.
To top it off, the movie is set to premiere May 9… during AAPI Heritage Month. @hallmarkchannel it’s not too late to scrap this mess.
#mahjong #movie #film #aapi #viral

Babe, wake up! Ranveer has designed his own adidas Originals Superstar. Superstar for THEE superstar!!
A Ranveer Singh for adidas Originals sneaker feels like a long time coming. The RGB colour-way track-sets, the bucket hats, the special collabs, and the plethora of shoes. Ranveer’s wardrobe choice and the Originals identity have merged over time to create a streetwear aesthetic that is instantly recognisable. He has sported a style that no one in his level of fame dares to tread, and the brand has served as a cultural lightning rod, bringing to him the best of global streetwear.
So finally when he gets to be a part of adidas India’s first ever statement shoe drop, it seems like a no-brainer. A MAJOR choice if you may. To collab at this level with Originals is not just an acknowledgment of Ranveer’s love for the brand, but also of his influence. Not influencer, baby, INFLUENTIAL. His ‘superstar’-dom, if you may. As they say, strike the iron while it’s hot, and adidas Originals has clearly clocked it, because if anyone can sell a shoe right now, it’s HIM.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There’s no forced “full circle” narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation — the same artist, only more settled into himself. “I don’t feel like I’m at such a different place in my career,” he says. “I feel like I’m at a different place and point as a human; I’m developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.” That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the 🔗in bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLECampaigns: Eka Summer ’26 unfolds in Sam, at the edge of the Thar Desert, where the landscape stretches until it almost disappears and then continues. Shot among shifting sand dunes, lone desert trees and open skies, the campaign leans into a sense of stillness and imagination, of journeys once taken across this terrain, of footsteps and hooves, of music carried through the night, of stories that linger in the air long after. There is a feeling of wandering to it, moving through the desert, pausing, observing, holding onto fragments and letting the rest drift.
This mood of movement and memory carries into Heat and Dust, where the desert becomes more than a backdrop, it becomes a language. The rhythm of dunes translates into soft stripes, checks and layered surfaces, while the palette reflects sun-faded sand, haldi, indigo and washed pastels. Airy silhouettes echo the way fabric responds to wind, and handwoven Kota with cotton silk holds these details lightly, allowing the collection to feel as fluid as the landscape it comes from.
A campaign that follows the rhythm of the desert.
Creative direction: Rina Singh @rinasing09

Since the September release of her hit sophomore album, ‘The Art of Loving,’ the British songstress has been riding a serious wave of momentum on both sides of the pond. Add in a Grammy win, standout live performances, a @cartier campaign, and critical raves, and it’s clear: Dean’s starpower is undeniable. As she prepares to kick off the album’s sold-out world tour, Dean is trying her best to soak it all in—and define success on her own terms.

posts.caption:
Ten years ago today, @beyonce’s ‘Lemonade’ reshaped culture—and the superstar’s legacy.
Most music released in 2016 seemed like a direct reflection of the joy and dissent that consumed the period. But no album quite defined the year like ‘Lemonade.’ Simply put, the album became a cultural phenomenon—one that poignantly centered on Black women’s pain and healing. Now, a decade later, ‘Lemonade’ has proven to be an album that was just as necessary for @beyonce’s career as it was for the Black women empowered by it.
At the link in bio, we take stock of the album’s resonance 10 years on.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
California released Amazon’s internal emails on Monday. They are worth reading carefully if you are a fashion brand that sells on the platform.
The short version: Amazon identified products listed at lower prices on Walmart and Target, contacted its vendors, and asked them to get those prices raised. The vendors complied. Levi Strauss confirmed Walmart had raised the price of its khaki trousers to $29.99 the same day Amazon asked. Hanes contacted both Walmart and Target directly after Amazon sent it links to cheaper competitor listings. California Attorney General Rob Bonta is calling this price-fixing. Read more in this column by Editor-in-Chief Anuj Kumar on fashionlawjournal.com

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Garment workers need our help by advocating further for change in fashion 💔⁠
⁠
For many workers throughout fashion’s supply chain, there is no guarantee of safe working conditions, proper payment, or benefits, all of which takes advantage of often vulnerable people as the biggest brand seek profits at the expense of persons they continue to take advantage of 😠⁠
⁠
A key way workers can create change is through collective bargaining, which allows workers and their employers to negotiate around their conditions. This is a fundamental element of autonomy—but not all brands are working to ensure this is the case, as many enable a problematic status quo.⁠
⁠
🚨 This is where you come in: by speaking up to brands and local governments, maintaining pressure to change the system, we can ensure workers worldwide are getting their due. Swipe to learn about this problem and how you can help. Explore the full story at the link ↑⁠
⁠

posts.caption: Ten years on, @Beyonce’s 2016’s "Lemonade" is still a masterpiece, and a win for modern fashion. What @chloessevigny stands for and what she’s stood for since the ’90s is a kind of preternatural authenticity, an unwavering sense of self. She became a natural emblem for the Alt girl. As Grunge took off, Sevigny realized, “Oh, people do think I’m something, and I want to take advantage of it in a way that is beneficial for me too.’” Even then, Sevigny knew that modeling would not give her the creative freedom and control over her career that she wanted for herself. “I remember doing the X-girl fashion show [in 1994] and seeing everybody in the audience [and thinking] I wanted to be one of the creative types that was invited to watch the show and not one of the girls walking it. It was a crystallizing moment for me.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
At Milan Design Week, fashion brands dress everything except the runway.⁠
⁠
This year, the real style flex comes from staff uniforms. Yohji Yamamoto teamed up with @aesop to create oversized, all-black jackets via his Y’s line, complete with crinkled “vintage” texture — luxury outerwear disguised as workwear. Meanwhile, LVMH Prize winner Soshiotsuki swapped tailoring for velour @asics tracksuits, blending retro suiting codes with athletic silhouettes in a sharp, cropped navy set.⁠
⁠
Both projects blur the line between uniform and collectible fashion, making everyday staff look like they’ve stepped out of a concept store campaign. If anything, they leave you wondering why these aren’t full collaborations — because honestly, who wouldn’t want in?

posts.caption:
@stoneisland distills decades of innovation into one sharp idea: one jacket, six identities.⁠
⁠
The new chapter of NO SEASONS, a concept first introduced by Massimo Osti in 1989, centers on a single outerwear silhouette reimagined through six signature fabrics: Tela Resinata, Nylon Prismatico-TC, Raso Gommato, David-TC, Crinkle Reps NY, and Panno. Each version is dyed in piombo, letting the character of each material subtly shift tone, texture, and depth while keeping the design constant.⁠
⁠
The result is a study in material science and construction, where fabric leads and form follows. The installation is open to the public from April 20 to 26, 10AM–8PM, at Capsule Plaza, Via Achille Maiocchi 8, Milan.

posts.caption:
What happens when you use a material famous for being as light as air to recreate a slab of solid rock? Meet “Airstone.”
Now on view and available to shop at Via Santa Marta 10 in Milan’s 5VIE district, the new Airstone tote bag is a design paradox.
Designed by @nichettostudio, the bag’s form was originally hand-carved from a block of rock before being 3D-scanned and injected with @XLEXTRALIGHT technology.
The result? A primitive-futuristic, limited-edition accessory that looks like a heavy, jagged artifact but weighs next to nothing. It’s a sensory mind-game that challenges how we perceive mass and material memory.
Sustainability isn’t an afterthought here, either.
The bag is crafted using XL EXTRALIGHT® Sustainable+, a blend featuring 51% post-industrial waste.
To launch the Airstone, XL EXTRALIGHT® has opened a popup installation which mixes acid-yellow accents, industrial metal cages, and sculptural foam rocks. It’ll have you wondering whether you’re in a high-end boutique or a geology lab.
The Airstone tote is available in three colorways exclusively at the Milan Design Week pop-up at Via Santa Marta 10.

posts.caption:
A Louisiana designer @noahvlaye created set made from “paper textile.” ⁠
⁠
The look mimics notebook paper through clever stitching — reddish-pink vertical lines paired with light blue horizontal ones — brought to life using layered white T-shirt fabrics, some faded or yellowed to evoke aged pages.⁠
⁠
Originally made two years ago, the piece is being revisited to highlight its concept and construction, offering a simple but inventive idea that others might build on or reinterpret.

posts.caption:
@naomigscott has spent years bringing other people’s stories to life. Now, with her debut album ‘F.I.G.’, she’s finally telling her own. From Disney teen to big screen star, she’s moved fluidly between worlds, always compelling but rarely the one in control of the narrative. Now the artist is stepping into her own, stripping everything back to offer an intimate and unfiltered look at who she is beyond the screen.
The album is a genre-fluid odyssey that moves between gospel warmth, ‘80s pop nostalgia, alt-pop edge and soulful ‘90s R&B. Made in a creative bubble that Scott describes as instinct-driven and free, it doesn’t sound like a debut so much as an arrival.
With her first North American tour on the horizon, we sat down with Scott to talk self-expression, musical identity and what it means to finally be seen as herself. Head to the link in bio to read the full interview.
Credits: @eloise_parry, Scott Jeremie Levy

Summer has a mood for every version of you—and @nidhhiagerwal knows exactly how to wear each one. Whether it’s the soft, golden haze of a slow morning or the unapologetic heat of high noon, she shifts between them the way only someone truly at ease in her own skin can. 🌸
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this becomes more than a seasonal study—it is a portrait of summer as emotion, texture, and transformation.
Light changes, moods shift, and she moves through it all with the kind of effortless cool that makes you want to rethink your entire wardrobe.
From dewy and dreamy to bold and sun-kissed, this is summer dressing as self-expression—textured, emotional, and completely her own.✨

Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
⁠While her approach to acting has always been instinctive and fluid, producing calls for a more structured mindset for @katehudson, who continues to expand her role behind the scenes as executive producer on ‘Running Point.’ ⁠
⁠
It’s also a natural next step in her career. Inspired by her mother, Goldie Hawn—one of the first actresses to produce her own films—Hudson explored producing early on, but stepped back after ‘Bride Wars,’ when she was a new mom and the timing didn’t feel right.⁠
⁠
As with everything else, she waited until she could fully commit—a quality that sets her up for a successful future as a producer. Read our full interview with Hudson at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Indian cricketer Jasprit Bumrah was recently seen wearing the ₹36-lakh @JacobAndCo Epic X ‘Sher-e-Punjab’ Edition, a distinctive timepiece that blends high-end watchmaking with strong cultural inspiration.
Created by Jacob & Co as a tribute to Sikh heritage, the watch features a lightweight 44mm titanium case and a skeletonised dial that reveals the intricate mechanics within. The vertical bridges are engraved with meaningful Sikh symbols, including the lion representing courage and leadership, the Khanda symbolising strength and unity, and the sacred Ek Onkar reflecting the belief in one universal God.
The titanium structure is paired with a bold blue honeycomb-textured rubber strap, a colour closely associated with Sikh identity. At its core is the hand-wound JCAM02 movement, offering a 48-hour power reserve while showcasing the watch’s detailed inner workings through the open dial.
Limited to just 50 pieces worldwide, the Sher-e-Punjab Edition honours the legacy of Guru Gobind Singh and the founding of the Khalsa, making it both a luxury watch and a tribute to heritage and resilience. ⌚✨

posts.caption:
Global icon Rihanna has made a surprise return to India, arriving in Mumbai nearly two years after her widely talked-about performance at the pre-wedding celebrations of Anant Ambani and Radhika Merchant in 2024. This time, her visit is tied to business rather than music, sparking fresh excitement among fans and industry watchers alike. ￼
The singer was spotted landing at Mumbai airport around midnight on April 24, dressed in an all-black look and greeting paparazzi with waves and smiles before heading to her car. Her sudden appearance quickly went viral online, with speculation building around the purpose of her visit. ￼
Reports suggest Rihanna is in India to attend a special event linked to her beauty label, Fenty Beauty. The event, titled “Fenty Beauty Ki Haveli,” is expected to spotlight the brand’s inclusive shade range and mark a key moment in its India expansion strategy. While full details remain under wraps, the visit signals growing interest from global beauty brands in the Indian market. ￼
Her return has once again placed India at the center of global pop culture attention, reinforcing how celebrity-led brand moments are increasingly shaping the country’s luxury and beauty landscape.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Judith Leiber Couture has officially entered the Indian market with the launch of its first boutique at The Chanakya in New Delhi, marking a significant step in the country’s expanding luxury retail landscape.
Known globally for its crystal-embellished minaudières and sculptural evening bags, the brand’s arrival reflects growing confidence in India as a key luxury destination. The boutique has been designed as an experience-led retail space, showcasing signature novelty minaudières, evening clutches, and seasonal collections that highlight the label’s craftsmanship and intricate detailing.
The move also signals a broader strategic push into emerging luxury markets, supported by rising numbers of high-net-worth individuals and increasing demand for statement craftsmanship over logo-driven fashion. Industry leaders view India as a long-term growth opportunity, with the Delhi launch serving as the foundation for the brand’s future expansion in the region.

From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy’s (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
“It wasn’t like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,” says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings — and one very intentional gym switch — finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
“I realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,” she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don’t need grand gestures to feel certain.

posts.caption:
This month, Vogue did something it had never done before, something most people thought it would probably never do: It put two 76-year-old women on its cover. Sure, they weren’t just any 76-year-olds. They were Meryl Streep and the Vogue overseer Anna Wintour, and they were there in a meta way to discuss the mythology around “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” in which Streep plays a version of Wintour. But still, 76.
The most recent round of fashion shows, which our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman attended last month, were notable not only for the almost total lack of size diversity on the runway, but also for the fact that they were, at the same time, taking one giant step forward when it came to another aspect of inclusivity: Age.
The Chanel show opened with 50-year-old Stephanie Cavalli, who was one of 15 models over 40 on its runway. Bottega Veneta had nine older models. Tom Ford, nine (women and men). Givenchy, eight. Balenciaga, five. Louis Vuitton, four. Representation of women of a certain age has never been higher. What’s going on and why now? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman delves into the striking shift.

posts.caption:
At Coachella last Friday afternoon, fans rushed to claim a spot in front of one of the festival stages. But the crowd wasn’t camping out for that night’s headliner, Sabrina Carpenter, or for the buzzy girl group Katseye. Attendees were patiently waiting to see Bob Baker Marionette Theater, home to some of Los Angeles’s most popular and beloved puppets.
Started in 1963 by a duo of puppeteers, Bob Baker and Alton Wood, the institution is one of the largest and longest-running puppet theaters in the U.S. But the theater has faced the prospect of closure several times over the past few decades, so performing at Coachella was a major moment for the group.
Mary Fagot, co-executive director of the theater, said that joining the festival lineup felt natural. “We were like, Of course you’re reaching out to us,” Fagot said with a laugh. “Coachella needs us to bring the real and the connected and the joy and the fun and the sense of not taking yourself too seriously.”
Get a closer look at the puppets' Coachella debut and learn more about Bob Baker Marionette Theater at the link in our bio. Visuals by @ye.fan and @dontgabalot

This week, not only did #Zendaya rewear her 2016 Oscars #VivienneWestwood gown, she also borrowed a Armani dress previously worn by the queen of outfit repetion, #CateBlanchett. It makes you think, should this become more common?
Bound by contracts, loyalty and evolving trends, celebrities have a responsibility to showcase new designers and support the fashion industry. Red carpets function as global advertising platforms providing exposure to talent, but should more celebs rewear their old clothes? By rewearing, new ways of styling and tailoring can transform an existing piece into something nostalgic yet completely new.
Most celebs often wear custom looks that are loaned to them ensuring each appearance feels exclusive and newsworthy. But once photographed and discussed, these garments (which take hundreds of hours to create) are archived. Yet in an increasingly sustainability-conscious landscape, should this change?
What do you think?
Images courtesy of Getty & Backgrid

One of the most striking looks was the floral dress, an adaptation of a painting by Filipino artist Olan Ventura. Featuring cascading strands of fringe which distorted the floral motifs across the dress so they appeared almost blurred or melting.
The headpieces, by @stephenjonesmillinery , were actually Silk T-shirts twisted and wrapped around the models’ heads, forming sculptural shapes reminiscent of Dutch Golden Age portraiture, specifically Rogier van der Weyden’s Woman with a Winged Bonnet.

posts.caption:
#Dior has been making some excellent red carpet looks recently and I can’t help but notice @jonathan.anderson’s revival of the Victorian bustle.
The bustle emerged in the late 19th century as a padded or structured undergarment worn beneath skirts to create volume at the back of the body, emphasizing a narrow waist. They relied on layers of fabric, pleating, and internal frameworks. Aptly linking #ChristianDior’s 1947 “New Look,” emphasising a cinched waist and structured hips.
#JonathanAnderson’s reinterpretation focuses on sculptural volume, creating shapes through draped panels, bows, or layered fabrics, creating extremely sculptural and romantic silhouettes. We’ve seen this in gowns, skirts, tailoring, and shorts, featuring pleated or structured volumes attached to the back of garments, expanding the concept of the bustle beyond traditional #HauteCouture.

posts.caption:
Could #Madonna’s stolen 2006 #Gucci jacket by #FridaGiannini spark a quiet revival? We all know and love #TomFord’s Gucci, but at this point it’s been overdone. Could Giannini’s era be the next interesting Gucci archive for true fashion girlies to pull from?
Frida had a difficult role stepping in after Ford, yet her version of Gucci brought stability and a different kind of glamour. This was the Gucci I grew up with and she made the brand feel polished and aspirational. A major part of her impact came through accessories, strengthening Gucci’s position as a leader in handbags and leather goods.
She loved jewel tones and metallics, and her references to the 1970s set the groundwork for what would come from #AlessandroMichele. While Michele pushed those references into maximalism and eccentricity, Frida’s work feels more archival than theatrical.
What did you think of Frida’s @gucci ?

posts.caption:
Seeing #ElsaSchiaparelli’s archive of perfumes at the @vamuseum made me realise…why are (most) luxury perfume bottles so boring now?
#Schiaparelli worked with creative collaborators such as Jean-Michel Frank, #SalvadorDalí, Leonor Fini, and Marcel Vertès to produce bottles and advertisements that were playful, imaginative, and sometimes shocking. Her most famous perfume, Shocking (1937), came in a bottle shaped like a woman’s torso, inspired by #MaeWest. Another example is Snuff, her only men’s perfume, designed in the shape of a pipe (see slide 11)
Today #perfume bottles today feel so minimal, focusing on consistent, recognisable designs. I guess clean, simple bottles are easier to produce, ship, and display, while complex shapes are more expensive to manufacture and more fragile to handle at scale. Culturally, contemporary luxury marketing often prioritises advertising and celebrity endorsements, shifting creativity away from the bottle itself.

Remember that viral #Loewe tomato bag? (Shoutout @conndo) Well, it seems #JonathanAnderson’s love for fruit and vegetables has left a lasting impression on Loewe’s designs. Their latest £4,000 leather artichoke bag makes you wonder, why are they so obsessed with fruit and vegetables?
These items aren’t just gimmicks; they’re demonstrations of Loewe’s craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand’s Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There’s also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That’s why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
Throughout history, women have made various efforts to make this world more just and sustainable. However, their initiatives were always treated as of secondary importance and did not receive much public recognition and support as they deserved.
India, a country which is known for its diversity in many aspects, including flora and fauna, has seen many women-led environmental movements. Indian women’s environmental engagement predates the formal label of ecofeminism; traditional roles in water management, agriculture and forest-based livelihoods made women custodians of ecological knowledge.
Ecofeminism in India is not only theoretical, but it is lived, practised, and continually negotiated in movements where gender, ecology, culture and community intersect. This Earth Day, take a look at some major women-led environmental movements.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full article by Ekta Singh, a PhD Scholar in the Discipline of Gender Studies at Banaras Hindu University. | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
We often witness mothers-in-law bestowing gold jewellery upon their daughters-in-law during weddings, often remarking how the piece is pushtaini (ancestral), once passed down to them by their own mothers-in-law. This tradition of passing down ornaments goes far beyond mere ritual. These heirlooms were originally given with love and care, intended to ensure the financial security of the women in the family. Even decades later, gold and property retain substantial value, safeguarding future generations against financial instability. Generations ago, gold wasn't just ornamental-they were a woman's rainy-day fund, a lifeline in times of uncertainty.
Unlike today, passing down property or gold wasn't a transaction or tool of control-it was a safeguard. According to the Arthashastra, a woman's ownership of Stridhana was legally recognised. Women had full rights to own, manage, and even dispose of their Stridhana, including the freedom to pass it on to their daughters or other relatives. In a society where men were traditionally the primary earners and women managed homes, Stridhana gave women a sense of financial agency and independence.
Before SIPs and stocks, our grandmothers saved in the form of gold. Today, in an age of credit cards, EMIs, and instant gratification, this wisdom remains quietly revolutionary. The most powerful legacy we inherit from the women before us isn't just the gold in our lockers-it's the foresight to prepare for life's uncertainties with grit and unwavering self-worth.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

posts.caption:
There is a scarcity around the rate of a fashion stylist. And there are a number of reasons for it.
We love gatekeeping. We do not want people knowing what we charge because we charge different clients different amounts. The industry is saturated with stylists undercutting. The top dogs who run the industry do not talk, will not share rates, and are generally stuck in their old ways. Their world is not affected, so they do nothing to help.
Fair enough.
But what about everyone else? What about the stylists in the middle? The ones who do not have the top celebrity clients. The ones who do not live in a fashion city. The up and coming ones who do not know what to charge. The ones who have been in it ten years and are still guessing.
That is who this is for❗️
Every stylist is different. Every client is different. Every job is different. Different rates in different sectors of styling. Different rates in different countries. Some countries have active unions helping stylists. Some do not. Some parts of the styling world are rate regulated. Some are not.
So no. This is not telling you what to charge.
This is how to work it out.
The Rate Build. Five parts. Run every time a new enquiry comes in.
Scope. Tier. Load. Extras. Minimum.
The framework is in the carousel. The data that goes with it is inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir, a paid membership community for fashion stylists. Rate guides built from data across every sector. Pitch frameworks. Portfolio teardowns. Sourcing systems. Contract templates. Community calls. Everything you need to price, pitch, protect, and grow a styling career.

posts.caption:
Undercharging does not just cost a stylist money.
It costs time. Unpaid prep days. Expenses absorbed. Hours working beyond the brief that never make it onto the invoice. It costs the right clients. The ones paying properly are not going to book a stylist whose rates suggest otherwise. It costs confidence. Every rate dropped takes a small part of the language to defend the next one. It costs relationships. The partner who watches the stylist work late again. The friends who stop getting replies. The family asking when this is going to start paying.
And it costs the industry. A generation of talented stylists leaving because the numbers never added up. Every stylist who walks away is a point of view the industry never got to keep.
The fee is not the only cost. It never has been.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is the membership platform for fashion stylists who are done absorbing what the industry will not pay for. Rate guides by sector. Editorial, commercial, celebrity. Scripts for when a client pushes back. Green lights for when to raise. Red flags for the clients to walk away from. And the community of stylists inside finally talking to each other about money, which is the part that actually changes things.
Built from what over 1,200 stylists told us they charge.

posts.caption:
Here’s the reality: too many clients treat estimates like free strategy sessions—then disappear the moment they see the price. Hours go into analyzing their market, understanding the moodboard, and shaping a direction, only to be met with silence when you ask for a budget. That cycle ends here.
From now on, the structure is simple, clear, and non-negotiable:
Minimum daily rate: $1000
Additional hours: $150/hour (minimum)
Props sourcing and styling: +$500/day
Full Art Direction (location scouting, coordination, creative oversight): +$1000/day
These are base rates—not premium, not luxury, just the starting point.
It’s also time to redefine the role of a stylist. This isn’t just about pulling looks. Styling is about building a visual narrative: concept development, moodboarding, sourcing, casting input, fittings, and on-set problem solving. It’s creative direction, strategy, logistics, and execution all working together to deliver a cohesive result.
Estimates are not free labor—they reflect time, expertise, and insight. If a client can’t share a budget upfront, they’re not ready to move forward.
Clear scope. Clear value. No more unpaid groundwork.

A momo isn’t just a ₹30 street snack. A Manipuri saree isn’t a ₹3,000 knockoff. Sacred shawls aren’t table runners.
When culture is stripped of context, representation slips into appropriation — and the cost runs deeper than money.
North East India is visible, yet still invisibilised. Newspaper coverage? Just 1.48% of space. OTT shows turn lived realities into “tribal survival” spectacles.
As Binita Chamling puts it: “When visibility itself is inaccurate, identity becomes fragile.” This is what erasure looks like — skill lost, nuance flattened, dignity compromised.
Representation requires context, respect, and care. Anything less reduces culture to shorthand.
It’s time to move beyond exoticising, and start paying attention.

Shalom Harlow was the 90s “anti-supermodel,” and that’s exactly why she defined the era. Discovered at a The Cure concert in 1990, she brought a completely different energy to the runway: less polished perfection, more instinctive, slightly offbeat cool. Designers like Versace and McQueen literally built moments around her presence.
She made high fashion feel effortless but never boring. Elegant, but with an edge. The kind of model who could carry a show without looking like she was trying to.
By 1995, she took home the industry’s first Model of the Year award, cementing what everyone already knew: she’s that girl.

posts.caption:
If you’re looking for a designer who treats fashion like theory, start with Hussein Chalayan. Often called fashion’s “mad professor,” Chalayan has never been interested in clothes as just clothes. His work sits somewhere between philosophy, science, and performance art—using garments to explore identity, migration, technology, and time itself.
He first broke through in 1993 with his graduate collection The Tangent Flows, where he buried silk dresses in a garden for three months to study decay, then sent them down the runway as living artifacts.
What sets Chalayan apart is that the technology in his work is never just for spectacle. Whether it’s transforming garments or conceptual staging, everything is in service of a deeper narrative about where we’ve been and where we’re going.
#HusseinChalayan #FashionHistory #ConceptualFashion #DesignThinking #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

Manoj Bajpayee’s (@bajpayee.manoj) filmography is a “mixed bag”, he points out—by design. His upcoming projects include ‘Governor’ directed by Chinmay Mandlekar, and a reunion with Ram Gopal Varma for the horror comedy ‘Police Station Mein Bhoot’. Neeraj Pandey’s crime thriller for Netflix is also in the pipeline, as is American director Ben Rekhi’s upcoming adaptation of Arvind Adiga’s novel ‘Last Man in Tower’.
Right now, Bajpayee is at that sweet spot in his career where he can take the long view. On screen, he has played losers and heroes, but IRL he’s able to balance celebrity with craft and Bollywood’s ruthless demands with his individual ambitions. For him, mainstream cinema is a strategic choice. “The Hindi film industry is very market-oriented, so to keep one’s value alive one has to balance it with things on which I don’t compromise,” Bajpayee explains.
