posts.caption:
Last year, designer and Madraswear founder Purushu Arie accused Chennai-based designer Vivek Karunakaran of copying his work—his innovations with tailored lungis, 𝐯𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐬 and a distinctly Tamil design language. Karunakaran maintained that his work was independently developed.
At 𝑻𝑽𝑶𝑭, we invited both to present their views—on design and copyright. The counterpoint, however, never ran. Because Arie’s argument moved beyond design authorship into caste, class, and privilege. We did not want not adjudicate the caste versus privilege terrain within a design plagiarism debate. I saw them as relevant but separate issues. Arie withdrew his article. We respected that decision.
Yet, part of what he wrote stayed with me. “My design authorship cannot be detached from its politics of assertion and resistance. Without that context, it is reduced to surface ornamentation—like temple colour schemes on Madras checks.”
Temple colours on Madras checks was a stinging metaphor. That what is worn on the street, shaped by stigma and social geography, is acceptable only after validation. Arie said he had been mocked for his work until validated by the press. Innovation from the margins he believes is often dismissed.
The argument knocks on a door that the fashion industry rarely opens.
April is Dalit History Month.
Yet, not much on caste has surfaced in Indian fashion media—not this month, not across the year. Indian fashion speaks fluently about craft. It debates cultural appropriation, now increasingly so, given the growing disregard to Indian-origin work and skills by Western brands. Textile-craft exhibitions foreground artisan visibility. But when it comes to caste—within which much of the labour exists—the conversation falls silent.
As Suraj Yengde writes in 𝑪𝒂𝒔𝒕𝒆 𝑴𝒂𝒕𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒔, caste is not a system waiting to disappear; it is graded hierarchy that mutates, and survives within modern institutions.
Fashion is no exception. Because it wears hierarchy on its sleeve. The lead image from Chamar Studio applauds its founder Sudheer Rajbhar for turning a slur into a brand. Read the rest on the link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork

posts.caption:
From the Ralph Lauren bandhani-print skirt and 𝒋𝒉𝒖𝒎𝒌𝒂 earrings, to Dior’s recent take on the 𝒃𝒂𝒏𝒅𝒉𝒈𝒂𝒍𝒂 and Prada’s Kolhapuri-inspired leather footwear—global fashion’s fascination with repackaging Indian crafts and textiles and giving them a six-figure price tag is peaking in the debates on cultural appropriation. As Ritu Sethi, founder-trustee of the Craft Revival Trust, points out: “Colonised nations have long been emporiums of patterns, designs and products that those in power have been picking and choosing from for many centuries. That is why the 𝒂𝒎𝒃𝒊 became the paisley, and you will see the ubiquitous ‘Indian prints’ or ‘African design’ in books.”
But in a market where motifs, techniques and narratives are copied and mass-produced—both by the domestic unorganised sector and international players— Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is what can separate authenticity from cultural extraction. Safir Anand, senior partner and head (Trademarks, Contractual and Commercial IP) at Delhi-based law firm Anand & Anand believes that the protection of Indian legacy often fails not because tools are unavailable, but because they are misunderstood.
This article, that first appeared in 𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑽𝒐𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝑭𝒂𝒔𝒉𝒊𝒐𝒏'𝒔 luxury white paper 𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑨𝒕𝒍𝒂𝒔 𝒐𝒇 𝑨𝒇𝒇𝒍𝒖𝒆𝒏𝒄𝒆 2026 on The Rise of Indian Luxury, examines how copyrights can safeguard creative expression and why filing for protection and taking legal recourse is no longer optional for Indian brands. Link in bio.

Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.

posts.caption:
I rewatched Anastasia and then spent three hours finding out everything I didn’t know about it as a kid. Fair warning: this film is not historically accurate, and by the end of these slides you’ll understand exactly why that matters.
Also how did we KNOW the blue dress was velvet without knowing it was velvet?
But first, Meg Ryan had to be convinced to take the role, Liz Callaway sang every song you love, and Jim Cummings voiced two of the most iconic villain songs of the 90s without getting nearly enough credit for either.
The further I went, the more complicated it got. The film is built on a rumour that was already disproven before it came out. It gave the revolution a face so the Romanovs didn’t have to have one. And Anastasia was canonised as a saint in 2000, a detail Western nostalgia tends to skip past entirely.
I still love this film. I’m just sitting with it differently now. What’s your favourite part? 🙊

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption: There are fashion obsessives, and then there’s @jeandenisfranoux. A longtime fashion professor at Studio Berçot and a designer himself from 1994 to 2001, Franoux began collecting perfume bottles as a child before getting hooked on clothes. Today, at 56, Franoux is sitting on an archive of 25,000 pieces and counting and from tonight through Saturday, an invitation-only event at a private space in the Upper Marais will offer a first look at the collective achievement, titled Regarderobes (the name is a portmanteau of the word “look” and “wardrobes”). Days before the opening, he welcomed Vogue to his Paris apartment/storeroom/makeshift photo studio, where hundreds of finds line racks while others, piled in boxes from floor to ceiling, await their close-up. Read more about what to expect at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 23, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, display case, mannequin and text.

posts.caption: With @GiorgioArmani’s passing in September 2025, he left behind an unwavering legacy of style. From his unstructured suits to his predominantly greige color palette, the Armani aesthetic—which is now being carried on by Silvana Armani and Leo Dell’Orco—is as recognizable and embraced in 2026 as it was in the designer’s heyday. Many big names in fashion spend their entire careers chasing an idea of timelessness; this second installment of the Armani/Archivio project only proves that Armani cemented his. “I believe there is nothing more radical than classics, because resisting the passage of time is, in fashion, a nearly revolutionary act,” Silvana Armani told Vogue. Learn more about the project at the link in bio.

Cast in a nostalgic mood that calls to mind the filmography of #DavidLynch and #StanleyKubrick, the actress transforms into a defiant, enigmatic persona as our 'Retrofuture' cover star, lensed by the legendary Canadian photographer @normanjeanroy. "For the shoot, we were tapping into a bit of a David Lynch world. It was so fun to lock into this set and character with the team on this shoot," Spaeny muses.
As one of two cover stars for April's 'Retrofuture' issue, Spaeny lets us into her universe—from her determined portrayal of Rain Carradine in 'Alien: Romulus' to the tender portrait she paints in 'Priscilla' and her newest work for @netflix's limited series 'Beef'. The full cover story, now in the link in bio.

posts.caption: The world’s oldest fine leather house @delvaux opens a new flagship at Hong Kong’s K11 MUSEA, weaving the Belgian maison’s storied heritage with a sharp, contemporary edge. While the facade carries art deco sensibilities, the interior defies the polished sameness that feels expected of luxury retail. In line with the maison’s affinity for the decorative arts as well as functional design that melds beauty with utility—elements that invite a slower, more thoughtful experience are infused throughout. posts.caption:
The Eagle-eyed Athlete. The Beauty Tycoon. The Homegrown Hotelier. Introducing: the remarkable women of #VogueLeaders 2026.
“I hope to use my platform to advocate for the importance of mental health and resilience in sports, helping athletes at all levels understand the value of mental well-being in achieving their full potential,” shares @amita_berthier, the two-time Olympic national fencer. Whilst Berthier leads in the field of sport, this year’s Vogue Leaders cohort also sees the induction of the Director of Estetica Beauty @karen.lamzy—who has frontiered Singapore’s aesthetics landscape—as well as Chief Operating Officer of Amara Holdings @dawn__teo, who stands behind one of the local hospitality brands.
Dressed in pieces from @maxmara, these women are seen in a study of presence—where dress moves beyond adornment to sit alongside identity, intention and becoming. Meet the 10 extraordinary women who are part of the 2026 class of Vogue Leaders, and discover their journeys via the link in bio—each offering a distinct perspective on what it means to be remarkable today. #VogueSingapore

𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝙨𝙝𝙞𝙠𝙖𝙧𝙜𝙖𝙝 𝙎𝙚𝙧𝙞𝙚𝙨 by @divaniindia
In the word “Shikargah” , “Shikar” means hunting and “Gah” means place.
The Shikargah by Divani is representative of a limited edition couture series reinterpreted with finest “ zardozi | ज़रदोज़ी | زردوزی " and the old school techniques of “tukdi ” patching using hand-dyed and handwoven tissue silk. From depicting the typical lion and deer hunt to british influences in designs can be notably seen as a part of the hunting scenes.
Featured here is our limited edition “Shikargah” saree, adorned with handcrafted animal and floral motifs on pure muga silk. The look is completed with an ornate jacket from the same series, featuring rich flora and fauna motifs rendered as an all-over pattern with archival techniques. The ensemble is further accentuated with a Shikargah "topi", highlighted by a striking “Bagh” motif at its centre.

posts.caption:
Rooted in the revival of royal costumes and heirloom traditions, this Farshi Garara traces back to the courts of Awadh and Hyderabad, known for its floor-length, sweeping silhouette.
Featured here is Divani’s classic Farshi Garara in a deep crimson tones, detailed with "lucknawi tukdi ka kaam" and crafted with pure copper wire zardozi craftsmanship. It is paired with a signature kurti, adorned with handcrafted peacock detailings. For the purposes of styling, the ensemble is completed with a "Hyderabadi khada dupatta", an heirloom drape from the royal wardrobes.
Crafted in pure silk tissue, this piece reflects the revival of handloom textiles through our Benarasi looms, bringing back the essence of royal dressings.

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There’s no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she’s stepping into her messiest role yet. “I was like, ‘Oh, that’s insane and feral. And I loved it,’” Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character’s marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig’s highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan’s real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption: Emilio Pucci’s L’ALBA Summer 2026 collection captures the softness of sunrise in Sicily fluid silks, vibrant prints, and effortless silhouettes. Designed by Camille Miceli, it blends bohemian ease with modern luxury, creating pieces that move with light and feel like summer itself.

posts.caption:
After 283 Grand Prix races, six wins, 39 podiums and three pole positions, Sergio “Checo” Pérez doesn’t have anything left to prove when it comes to his @F1 racing career — but everything left to build.⁠
⁠
Now leading the charge at the Cadillac Formula 1 Team, the Mexican superstar is turning a fresh start into a final statement. ⁠
⁠
"I had unfinished business," he said in WWD's April Weekend cover story. ⁠
Back on the grid and in @TommyHilfiger, Sergio Pérez steps into a new era. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Forget quiet luxury — spring 2026 is all about Marie Antoinette.
Twenty years after Sofia Coppola’s iconic biopic, the queen of excess is back in fashion, inspiring corsets, crinolines and rococo details across the runways from Dior to Junya Watanabe.
In Paris, the @palaisgallieramuseedelamode latest exhibition, “Fashion in the 18th Century: A Fantasized Legacy,” even showcases Marie Antoinette’s famed corset alongside designs by Karl Lagerfeld, John Galliano and Vivienne Westwood.
Swipe to see and read more in the April issue of WWD Weekend at the link in bio.
Report and video: @jdiderich

posts.caption:
Stars including Dakota Johnson, Victoria and David Beckham, Jennie and more took to Jazz at Lincoln Center to celebrate the @Time 100 list.
Victoria and David Beckham opted to skip the red carpet, instead slinking in through cocktails and into the ballroom at the last minute. The other honorees and their plus ones, however, spent the earlier portion of the night posing for photos and mingling about.
Read everything that happened inside at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @leighnordstrom
Photos: @lexieblacklock / WWD

posts.caption:
Saudi Arabia’s first private equity fund dedicated to the fashion sector now has a name and a public identity to match its ambitions.
ZYA Fund, the $80 million investment vehicle anchored by the Cultural Development Fund and managed by Merak Capital, has unveiled its brand identity and convened the inaugural meeting of its board of directors.
The fund was announced at the Cultural Investment Conference and represents, in the words of CDF chief executive officer Majed Al-Hugail, “the first time a dedicated fashion investment vehicle in the kingdom carries a distinct public identity.”
Read all about it at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @rituupadhyay
